4 min read
1 hour ago

--

Every Express application eventually needs rate limiting.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Whether you’re protecting authentication endpoints, preventing spam, or simply stopping accidental abuse, rate limiting is one of those features that becomes essential the moment your API goes public.
When I started looking for a rate limiter that matched my needs, I found plenty of excellent libraries. But I also realized many projects only need something lightweight, easy to understand, and simple to extend.
That’s why I built req-guard-lite.
Why Another Rate Limiter?
Most APIs don’t need an enterprise-level security framework on day one.
Sometimes you just want to write:
app.use(rateLimit({
max: 100,
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000
}));…and move on.
My goal was to create a package that is:

• Lightweight

• Easy to configure

• TypeScript-first

• Extensible

• Suitable for both small and large deployments

Introducing req-guard-lite
req-guard-lite is a lightweight Express middleware that protects your API from excessive requests.
It works out of the box using an in-memory store, but can also scale to distributed deployments using Redis.
The package focuses on doing one thing well:
Count requests and block clients that exceed your configured limit.

Features
✅ Lightweight implementation
✅ Zero runtime dependencies on the main package
✅ Express middleware
✅ TypeScript support
✅ Redis support
✅ Custom storage backends
✅ Custom key generators

Getting Started
Install it alongside Express.
npm install req-guard-lite expressThen add it to your application.
import express from 'express';
import { rateLimit } from 'req-guard-lite';const app = express();
const limiter = rateLimit({
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000,
max: 100,
message: 'Too many requests, please try again later.'
});
app.use(limiter);
app.get('/', (req, res) => {
res.send('Hello World!');
});
app.listen(3000);
That’s it.
Your API is now protected from clients making more than 100 requests within 15 minutes.

Default In-Memory Store
The default implementation stores counters in memory.
This means:

• No Redis

• No database

• No configuration

• Perfect for development

• Great for single-server production deployments
For many applications, that’s all you’ll ever need.

Scaling with Redis
Once your application grows and runs behind multiple servers or containers, each server needs to share request counts.
That’s where Redis comes in.
import Redis from "ioredis";
import { createRedisStore } from "req-guard-lite/redis";const redis = new Redis();
const limiter = rateLimit({
max: 100,
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000,
store: createRedisStore(redis, {
max: 100,
windowMs: 15 * 60 * 1000
})
});
Every server now uses the same counters, making rate limiting consistent across your infrastructure.

Custom Key Generators
Not every API should rate limit by IP address.
Sometimes you want limits based on:

• User ID

• API Key

• Tenant

• Organization

• JWT Subject

• Anything else
That’s why req-guard-lite lets you define your own key generator.
const limiter = rateLimit({
max: 100,
keyGenerator: (req) =>
req.headers["x-api-key"] as string
});Or by authenticated user:
const limiter = rateLimit({
max: 50,
keyGenerator: (req) =>
(req as any).user.id
});The middleware doesn’t care what your key is — it simply counts requests for whatever identifier you provide.

Bring Your Own Store
One design goal from the beginning was extensibility.
Instead of forcing Redis, the package exposes a simple RateLimitStore interface.
If you want to store counters in:

• PostgreSQL

• DynamoDB

• Memcached

• MongoDB

• SQLite

• Your own caching layer
You simply implement one interface.
class MyStore implements RateLimitStore {
consume(key: string) {
// your implementation
}
}This makes the package flexible enough for almost any infrastructure.

One Important Production Tip
If you’re deploying behind:

• Nginx

• AWS Load Balancer

• Heroku

• Cloudflare

• Reverse proxies
Don’t forget:
app.set("trust proxy", 1);Without this, Express may think every request comes from the proxy itself, causing all users to share the same rate limit.
It’s a small configuration that prevents a surprisingly common production issue.

How It Works
The flow is intentionally simple.

• A request arrives.

• A unique key is generated (IP by default).

• The configured store increments the request count.

• If the limit is exceeded, the middleware returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests.

• Otherwise, the request continues normally.
The storage backend can be in-memory, Redis, or any custom implementation.

Why TypeScript?
The package is written entirely in TypeScript.
That means:

• Strong typing

• Better IntelliSense

• Easier maintenance

• Safer APIs
Consumers using TypeScript get full type definitions without installing anything extra.

Roadmap
The project is still growing.
Here’s what’s planned next.
v0.4.0

• Standard Rate Limit response headers
X-RateLimit-Limit
X-RateLimit-Remaining
X-RateLimit-ResetThese headers help clients understand how many requests they have left before hitting the limit.
v0.5.0
Custom hooks when limits are exceeded.
For example:

• Logging

• Metrics

• Alerting

• Analytics

• External monitoring services

Why I Open Sourced It
This wasn’t built because existing libraries are bad.
There are already fantastic solutions in the Node ecosystem.
I built req-guard-lite because I wanted a package that was:

• Small enough to understand in one sitting

• Easy to extend

• TypeScript-first

• Free from unnecessary complexity

• Flexible enough to grow with real-world applications
It has also been a great learning experience in package publishing, API design, testing, Redis integration, and building developer-friendly abstractions.

Final Thoughts
Open source is one of the best ways to learn software engineering.
Building something that other developers can install, use, and contribute to forces you to think beyond your own project. Documentation, API design, testing, versioning, and backward compatibility all become part of the challenge.
req-guard-lite started as a simple middleware for my own projects, but I hope it becomes useful to other Express developers looking for a lightweight and extensible rate limiting solution.
If you have ideas, feature requests, or want to contribute, I’d love to hear your feedback.
Happy coding!
